statement: Docker Compose services on the same network communicate by service name (not localhost). If 'web' and 'db' are services, web connects to 'db:5432', not 'localhost:5432'. UNSAID: a common mistake is hardcoding 'localhost' in app configs inside containers — this refers to the container itself, not the host or sibling containers. Port mapping (ports:) makes services reachable from the HOST; internal communication uses the Docker internal DNS. Extraction signal: database URLs with 'localhost' inside a Docker Compose setup are misconfigured.

Self-hosted applications typically sit behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, Caddy) that handles TLS termination, routing, and load balancing. The app receives HTTP traffic (not HTTPS) from the proxy. UNSAID: X-Forwarded-For header carries the real client IP — apps must trust and read this header to log real IPs. Without proper trusted-proxy configuration, all requests appear to come from the proxy. Caddy auto-provisions Let's Encrypt certs; Traefik does the same with Let's Encrypt/ZeroSSL. Extraction signal: apps that hardcode port 443 or handle their own TLS inside Docker are fighting against the standard reverse-proxy model.

Docker containers are ephemeral — all data written inside a container is lost on container restart unless mounted to a volume or bind mount. Named volumes (managed by Docker) vs bind mounts (mapped to host directory) serve different needs. UNSAID: SQLite databases INSIDE a container (not volume-mounted) will lose all data on container updates. This is the #1 data loss cause in self-hosted deployments. Extraction signal: any database file path in a Docker container not pointing to a volume mount will lose data on container replacement.

Docker Compose .env files are readable by all containers in the compose project by default via ${VAR} substitution. Docker secrets (Swarm mode) provide encrypted secret storage. UNSAID: .env files committed to git repositories expose secrets — this is the most common credential leak. .env files should be in .gitignore; provide .env.example instead. Environment variables in Docker are visible in process listings and docker inspect — not suitable for high-sensitivity secrets (use Docker secrets or a vault like HashiCorp Vault). Extraction signal: hardcoded passwords in docker-compose.yml are critical security issues.

Docker health checks (HEALTHCHECK instruction or healthcheck: in compose) enable depends_on: condition: service_healthy to ensure the DB is ready before the app starts. Without healthchecks, depends_on: only waits for the container to START, not to be READY. UNSAID: the classic race condition — app container starts before DB is accepting connections — causes 'connection refused' errors that are hard to debug. Standard fix: healthcheck + condition: service_healthy, or a wait-for-it.sh script. Extraction signal: projects with startup order issues that use sleep 10 as a workaround need proper healthchecks.

Self-hosted data requires a backup strategy: volume snapshots, pg_dump for PostgreSQL, SQLite backup API for SQLite, restic/rclone for file backups. 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite. UNSAID: backing up a PostgreSQL data directory while the DB is running can produce corrupt backups unless using pg_dump (logical backup) or pg_basebackup (physical, with WAL). Extraction signal: self-hosted projects without documented backup procedures are one disk failure away from data loss.

Kubernetes pod scheduling is controlled by three mechanisms that work together: nodeSelector (label equality match), nodeAffinity (rich expression matching), and taints/tolerations (node repulsion). UNSAID: nodeSelector is simpler but nodeAffinity with requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution gives you AND/OR logic across multiple labels. Taints mark nodes as 'polluted' (e.g., taint key=gpu:NoSchedule) and only pods with matching tolerations can land there — useful for dedicating nodes to GPU or system workloads. Inter-pod affinity (podAffinity/podAntiAffinity) co-locates or spreads pods across topology zones. A common mistake is over-specifying nodeAffinity so tightly that Pending pods cannot be scheduled because no node matches — always test with kubectl describe pod to see the Events section explaining why a pod is stuck. Extraction signal: pods in Pending state with 'no nodes matched' events indicate overly restrictive scheduling constraints.

Kubernetes resource limits (requests vs limits) are the most misunderstood tuning knob. Requests are what the scheduler uses to place the pod; limits are the enforcement ceiling. UNSAID: setting CPU limits causes CPU throttling even when the node has idle capacity — many teams remove CPU limits and only set CPU requests. Memory limits, however, should always be set: a pod exceeding its memory limit is OOMKilled immediately. Setting requests == limits (Guaranteed QoS) means pods are never OOMKilled by node pressure but waste capacity. Setting no limits at all (BestEffort QoS) means the pod is first to be evicted when the node is under pressure. Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) can recommend right-sized values based on historical usage. Extraction signal: CrashLoopBackOff pods with OOMKilled reason need higher memory limits; CPU-throttled services need limits removed or raised.

Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales the number of pod replicas based on metrics. The default metric is CPU utilization; custom metrics (RPS, queue depth, latency) require the Kubernetes Metrics Server plus a custom metrics adapter (Prometheus Adapter or KEDA). UNSAID: HPA and VPA should not both target CPU on the same Deployment — they fight each other. Use HPA for scaling out (replicas) and VPA in Off mode just for recommendations, or use KEDA which unifies event-driven scaling. Scale-down is intentionally slow (default cooldown 5 min) to avoid flapping; scale-up is fast. Extraction signal: services that spike and drop quickly need a shorter scaleDown stabilizationWindowSeconds; services that remain over-provisioned need VPA recommendations applied.

Kubernetes Ingress exposes HTTP/HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to services. Ingress controllers (nginx-ingress, Traefik, HAProxy, Contour) implement the Ingress spec. UNSAID: the Ingress resource alone does nothing — you need an Ingress controller deployed in the cluster. cert-manager integrates with Ingress via the kubernetes.io/tls annotation and automatically provisions Let's Encrypt TLS certificates using ACME HTTP-01 or DNS-01 challenges. Multiple Ingress resources with the same hostname but different paths are merged by the controller — conflicting rules between namespaces can cause unexpected routing. For gRPC or WebSocket traffic, ensure the Ingress controller is configured with appropriate timeouts and protocol support, as default HTTP/1.1 proxying breaks both. Extraction signal: services accessible inside the cluster but unreachable externally need Ingress + controller + DNS configuration verified.

Kubernetes RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) controls what API operations subjects (users, groups, service accounts) can perform on which resources. Roles are namespace-scoped; ClusterRoles span the whole cluster. UNSAID: the most common RBAC mistake is granting cluster-admin to application service accounts 'to make it work' — this gives the pod the ability to read all secrets, delete any resource, and escalate privileges. Principle of least privilege: application pods should only have the ServiceAccount permissions they actually need (e.g., only list/watch ConfigMaps in their own namespace). Audit RBAC grants with kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa>. Wildcard verbs ('*') and resources ('*') are red flags in production RoleBindings. Extraction signal: pods with cluster-admin ServiceAccount are a critical security finding in any Kubernetes deployment.

Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricts pod-to-pod traffic at the network layer. Without any NetworkPolicy, all pods in the cluster can reach all other pods — a flat, fully open network. UNSAID: NetworkPolicy is only enforced if the CNI plugin supports it (Calico, Cilium, Weave Net support it; Flannel alone does NOT enforce NetworkPolicy). A default-deny-all policy in each namespace followed by explicit allow rules is the gold standard. Services like databases should only accept connections from their application pods, not from the entire cluster. Extraction signal: clusters using Flannel as the only CNI have no network isolation even if NetworkPolicy resources exist — they are silently ignored.

Kubernetes Secret management has a critical flaw: Secrets are base64-encoded (not encrypted) by default in etcd. Sealed Secrets (Bitnami) or External Secrets Operator (ESO) with a backend like HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or GCP Secret Manager provide real encryption-at-rest. UNSAID: committing Kubernetes Secret YAML files to git exposes base64-decoded values immediately — base64 is not encryption. Sealed Secrets encrypts with the cluster's public key so the encrypted SealedSecret YAML is safe to commit; only the cluster's controller can decrypt it. ESO pulls secrets at pod start time from an external vault, keeping the source of truth outside the cluster entirely. Rotation is easier with ESO because updating the external secret automatically refreshes the Kubernetes Secret. Extraction signal: Kubernetes Secret YAML files present in git repositories are a critical security finding.

Kubernetes Persistent Volumes (PV) and PersistentVolumeClaims (PVC) decouple storage provisioning from pod lifecycle. StorageClasses enable dynamic provisioning — when a PVC is created, the CSI driver automatically provisions the underlying volume. UNSAID: the reclaim policy matters enormously: Retain keeps the volume after PVC deletion (manual cleanup required, data safe); Delete removes the volume immediately (data gone). Default StorageClasses in many distributions use Delete — migrating a stateful app without setting the reclaim policy to Retain can cause permanent data loss when the PVC is accidentally deleted. ReadWriteOnce (RWO) volumes can only be mounted by one node; ReadWriteMany (RWX) requires NFS or a distributed filesystem (CephFS, NFS-subdir). Extraction signal: StatefulSets using the default StorageClass without explicitly setting Retain reclaim policy are at risk of data loss on PVC deletion.

Prometheus + Grafana is the de facto observability stack for Kubernetes. kube-prometheus-stack (Helm chart) deploys Prometheus Operator, Grafana, Alertmanager, and pre-built dashboards for Kubernetes cluster metrics in one command. UNSAID: Prometheus scrapes metrics by convention via /metrics endpoints and ServiceMonitor CRDs — adding a ServiceMonitor is how you onboard a new service into monitoring. Cardinality explosion is the #1 Prometheus operational failure: high-cardinality labels (user IDs, request IDs, full URLs) in metrics cause memory exhaustion. AlertManager deduplicates, groups, and routes alerts to PagerDuty/Slack — without proper grouping, a cluster failure triggers thousands of individual alerts (alert storm). Long-term storage requires remote_write to Thanos, Cortex, or Mimir since Prometheus local storage is not designed for multi-year retention. Extraction signal: Prometheus pods with OOMKilled status are usually caused by high-cardinality metric labels.

GitOps is the practice of using Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and application configuration. ArgoCD and Flux are the two dominant Kubernetes GitOps controllers. UNSAID: the core GitOps invariant is that the cluster state must always converge to match the Git state — any manual kubectl apply that is not reflected in Git will be overwritten by the next sync. This 'drift detection and reconciliation' is a feature, not a bug, but teams accustomed to manual ops find it jarring. ArgoCD provides a visual UI for sync status; Flux is more CLI-centric and integrates tightly with Helm and Kustomize. Separation of concerns: application code lives in one repo; Kubernetes manifests live in a separate 'gitops' repo — this allows independent audit trails and finer access control. Image update automation (Flux Image Reflector) can automatically open PRs to update image tags when a new image is pushed. Extraction signal: clusters where production changes are applied via ad-hoc kubectl commands are not in a GitOps model and lack an audit trail.

Multi-tenancy in Kubernetes comes in three models: namespace-per-tenant (soft isolation, shared control plane), virtual clusters (vcluster — tenant gets their own API server within the host cluster), and separate physical clusters (full isolation, high cost). UNSAID: namespace isolation is incomplete without NetworkPolicy (default-deny), LimitRanges (prevent resource hogging), ResourceQuotas (cap total namespace consumption), and RBAC (prevent cross-namespace access). Kubernetes does not prevent a tenant's pod from accessing another tenant's PVC by volume name if they know it — physical cluster separation is the only true hard isolation. Hierarchical namespaces (HNC) let platform teams create namespace hierarchies with inherited policies. Cost showback/chargeback per tenant requires namespace-level resource tagging and a tool like OpenCost or Kubecost. Extraction signal: shared clusters with per-namespace tenants but no ResourceQuota will have noisy-neighbor resource contention.

Kubernetes log aggregation requires shipping container logs (which are ephemeral, stored on the node in /var/log/containers/) to a centralized store before pods are terminated or nodes are cycled. The standard stack is Fluent Bit (lightweight DaemonSet) → Elasticsearch or Loki, visualized in Kibana or Grafana. UNSAID: Loki (Grafana Labs) stores only log metadata as index and the raw log stream compressed — dramatically cheaper than Elasticsearch for pure log storage. However, Loki does not support full-text search across all fields; you must query by label first. Fluent Bit uses less memory than Fluentd and is preferred for Kubernetes DaemonSet deployments. Log volume from verbose DEBUG-level application logs can overwhelm the pipeline — enforce log level discipline (INFO in production, DEBUG behind a feature flag). Extraction signal: clusters without a log aggregation DaemonSet lose all logs from terminated pods, making post-incident diagnosis impossible.
